does: This paper introduces Med-RewardBench, the first benchmark designed specifically to evaluate reward models and judges for medical multimodal large language models. The benchmark comprises a multimodal dataset with 1,026 expert-annotated cases spanning 13 organ systems and 8 clinical departments, constructed through a rigorous three-step validation process. The authors evaluate 32 state-of-the-art MLLMs across six clinically critical evaluation dimensions, establishing baseline performance metrics for assessing how well reward models and judges can evaluate medical MLLM responses. The benchmark addresses a gap in evaluating alignment with clinical requirements rather than focusing solely on general capability metrics.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on alignment and evaluation of large language models, building on prior research that has focused primarily on general-domain reward models and benchmarks like those used for instruction-following and harmlessness. It responds to limitations in existing MLLM evaluation frameworks, which typically measure solving ability rather than response quality along clinically relevant dimensions. The paper connects to earlier work on medical AI evaluation and diagnostic accuracy assessment, while also drawing on recent advances in multimodal LLMs and their application to medical domains. It acknowledges that specialized evaluation is necessary for high-stakes applications, a principle established in medical AI research but underexplored in the reward modeling literature.
enables: This benchmark enables future development of specialized medical reward models that can reliably score clinical decision quality, diagnostic accuracy, and professional alignment in medical LLM outputs. By providing standardized evaluation data and establishing baseline performance, it supports research into improving reward model training for clinical applications and developing better judges for medical decision-making systems. The work opens pathways for clinical deployment of MLLMs by creating mechanisms to measure and ensure response quality in high-stakes medical contexts. It also establishes a foundation for evaluating reinforcement learning from human feedback specifically tailored to medical settings, potentially advancing safer and more trustworthy AI systems in healthcare.
